studentTips.title: Work During Off-Peak Hours
studentTips.description: Use early mornings, evenings, and weekends when you're not in class.

studentTips.title: Combine Study with Work
studentTips.description: Listen to lectures while doing data entry or use study breaks for quick tasks.

studentTips.title: Use University Resources
studentTips.description: Many universities have job boards and career centers specifically for students.

studentTips.title: Communicate Your Schedule
studentTips.description: Be upfront with employers about your class schedule and exam periods.

studentTips.title: Prioritize Academics
studentTips.description: Work is important, but your degree comes first. Don't overcommit.

studentTips.title: Build Relevant Experience
studentTips.description: Choose jobs related to your major to build your resume while earning.

studentStories.name: Alex
studentStories.story: I tutor computer science students online. The hours are flexible, and I'm reinforcing what I learn in class.

studentStories.name: Jessica
studentStories.story: I manage social media for 3 local cafes. It takes 10 hours/week and fits perfectly around my classes.

studentStories.name: Michael
studentStories.story: Freelance writing for tech blogs. I work 20 hours/week and it's directly related to my journalism degree.

tips.tip: Use 30-60 minutes before your first class for quick paid tasks
2-hour breaks are perfect for focused work or tutoring sessions
Work 2-3 hours after classes - most productive time for many students
Dedicate 4-6 hours on Saturday or Sunday for bigger projects

taxTips.title: Track Your Earnings
taxTips.description: Keep records of all income - you'll need this for taxes.

taxTips.title: Know Your Allowance
taxTips.description: Many countries have tax-free allowances for students. Research yours!

taxTips.title: Save for Taxes
taxTips.description: Set aside 15-20% of each payment for potential taxes.

taxTips.title: Deduct Expenses
taxTips.description: You may deduct work-related expenses like internet or equipment.

faq.title: Earn Money as a Student FAQ - {country}

items.question: How many hours can a student work?
items.answer: Most students work 10-20 hours per week during school and up to 40 hours during breaks. Check your visa restrictions if international.

items.question: Can international students work online?
items.answer: It depends on your visa. Many student visas allow part-time work (usually 20 hours/week). Check with your international student office.

items.question: What's the best job for a student with no experience?
items.answer: Data entry, virtual assistant, and online surveys are great starting points. Tutoring in subjects you're good at also works well.

items.question: How do I balance work and studying?
items.answer: Create a schedule, use a planner, communicate with employers about exam periods, and don't overcommit. Your grades come first!

items.question: Can I work from my dorm room?
items.answer: Absolutely! Most online student jobs only require a laptop and internet connection.

items.question: Do I need to tell my university I'm working?
items.answer: Not usually for online freelance work, but check your university's policies. International students must follow visa work restrictions.

items.question: How much can a student realistically earn?
items.answer: Most students earn $800-2,000/month working 10-20 hours/week. Some earn more with specialized skills like coding or design.

final.title: Start Earning While You Learn
final.subtitle: Join thousands of students already earning from home
